summary: Gruber builds on his earlier iPad Mini analysis by working through the likely physical design of the device, arguing it won't simply be a shrunken iPad but will have its own distinct proportions — specifically narrower side bezels giving it more of an iPhone-like forehead-and-chin layout. He examines leaked parts showing a device aspect ratio near 3:2 despite a 4:3 display, suggesting Apple will minimize side bezels. He calculates that the iPad Mini could match the Nexus 7's footprint while offering a larger display, and estimates it will be remarkably thin (7.2mm, iPod Touch-thin) and light (~265 grams). He argues Apple strategically leaked the iPad Mini's existence to Bloomberg and the WSJ precisely when Nexus 7 reviews dropped, to preemptively dampen competitor momentum. Combined with cellular networking, a rear camera, the App Store, and worldwide availability, Gruber concludes Apple is well-positioned in the small tablet market.
key_points: Apple product designs have proportions true to their own nature — the iPad Mini won't simply be a shrunken iPad but will have narrower side bezels and different device-to-display aspect ratios
Leaked parts consistently show a device aspect ratio near 3:2, which combined with a 4:3 display implies thin side bezels and thick top/bottom bezels — an iPhone-like forehead-and-chin design
Apple strategically leaked iPad Mini news to Bloomberg and the WSJ within a day of each other, precisely timed to the Nexus 7's launch to preemptively frame it as a competitive threat
The iPad Mini could match the Nexus 7's physical size while offering a bigger display — playing the 'bigger display is better' card without the tradeoff of a larger device
By forgoing a retina display, Apple gains the ability to make the device remarkably thin (~7.2mm) and light (~265g), which matters enormously for one-handed use
money_quotes: Apple product designs are true to themselves. Each thing has proportions suited to its own nature.

If you think these stories appearing within a day of each other in the two most-respected business publications in the U.S. — at the same time the Nexus 7 reviews began appearing and the device started shipping to customers — is merely coincidental and not a strategic competitive leak from Apple PR, then I would like to invite you to play in my poker game.
Here, Apple would be doing to the Nexus what Android phones have been doing to the iPhone — playing the 'bigger display is better' card.
But unlike with phones, here Apple would be playing the 'bigger display is better' card without the corresponding tradeoff in overall device size, where smaller (and lighter) are better.
Weight is a huge factor, maybe the factor, in one-handed use, and its too-heavy-to-hold-in-one-hand weight has always been one of the biggest knocks against the iPad-as-we-know-it.
Maybe iPad Mini is the wrong name for us to use as a placeholder. It's not about being smaller — it's about being thinner and lighter. iPad Air?
Apple, to date, has never introduced a new iOS form factor with a retina display — they add retina displays as upgrades two or three years after devices hit the market.
key_insight: The iPad Mini's competitive advantage won't come from being a shrunken iPad but from Apple's willingness to rethink bezel proportions, enabling a device that matches competitors' size while offering a larger display and being dramatically thinner and lighter.
